coredata.dc:description:
Mercury is a hazardous heavy metal that poses serious threats to human health and the environment. Hence, developing simple, rapid, cost-effective, and portable analytical methods with high sensitivity and selectivity is critical. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step microwave process using urea and citric acid, achieving a quantum yield of 45.03%. The CQDs were characterized by UV–Vis, PL, XRD, XPS, FTIR, and TEM techniques. They exhibited highly selective, interference-free fluorescence detection of Hg
2 +
ions within 7 min, even in the presence of other metal ions (Mg
2 +
, Ca2＋, Mn
2 +
, Fe2＋, Co
2 +
, Ni
2 +
, Cu
2 +
, Zn
2 +
, Cd
2 +
, Pb
2 +
, Al3＋, Fe3＋, and Na
+
). A linear fluorescence response was observed in the Hg
2 +
concentration range of 2–9 μ M (R
2
= 0 . 99861
), with a detection limit as low as 1.8 nM. The CQDs maintained stable detection performance in a pH range of 6–9. Furthermore, a simple paper-based sensor was developed for on-site mercury detection. The sensing mechanism was explored through FTIR, UV–Vis, TEM, and a novel agarose gel electrophoresis technique, which revealed the migration behavior of CQD-Hg
2 +
complexes at varying concentrations. This study offers a highly selective, rapid, and practical mercury detection method with enhanced LOD and provides mechanistic insights into CQD-Hg
2 +
interactions, using gel electrophoresis to deepen the understanding of the sensing process.

head.ce:title: Enhanced and rapid detection of mercury ions using one-step microwave synthesized fluorescent carbon quantum dots: Development of a very simple paper-based sensor with detailed mechanistic insights

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Mercury is a hazardous heavy metal that poses serious threats to human health and the environment. Hence, developing simple, rapid, cost-effective, and portable analytical methods with high sensitivity and selectivity is critical. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a one-step microwave process using urea and citric acid, achieving a quantum yield of 45.03%. The CQDs were characterized by UV–Vis, PL, XRD, XPS, FTIR, and TEM techniques. They exhibited highly selective, interference-free fluorescence detection of Hg
2 +
ions within 7 min, even in the presence of other metal ions (Mg
2 +
, Ca2＋, Mn
2 +
, Fe2＋, Co
2 +
, Ni
2 +
, Cu
2 +
, Zn
2 +
, Cd
2 +
, Pb
2 +
, Al3＋, Fe3＋, and Na
+
). A linear fluorescence response was observed in the Hg
2 +
concentration range of 2–9 μ M (R
2
= 0 . 99861
), with a detection limit as low as 1.8 nM. The CQDs maintained stable detection performance in a pH range of 6–9. Furthermore, a simple paper-based sensor was developed for on-site mercury detection. The sensing mechanism was explored through FTIR, UV–Vis, TEM, and a novel agarose gel electrophoresis technique, which revealed the migration behavior of CQD-Hg
2 +
complexes at varying concentrations. This study offers a highly selective, rapid, and practical mercury detection method with enhanced LOD and provides mechanistic insights into CQD-Hg
2 +
interactions, using gel electrophoresis to deepen the understanding of the sensing process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury, present in organic, inorganic and elemental forms, is one of the most toxic metals, posing threats to the environment and human health. Mercury causes damage to the alimentary canal, liver and kidneys and poses risks of depression, paralysis, Alzheimer’s, and potential carcinogenic effects. Particularly harmful to the developing fetus, mercury also adversely affects the cardiovascular system [1]. Naturally occurring in oil and gas reservoirs [2], mercury is widely used across industries, including insecticides, agriculture products, lamps, batteries, paper, dyes, electronics, jewelry, and dentistry [3]. As a result, mercury contamination poses a high risk at extraction and application sites. Historical incidents, such as the mercury poisoning epidemics in Japan (Minamata Bay and the Agano River) between 1953 and 1960, underscore the gravity of the issue [4]. Current trends indicate that mercury pollution remains a significant environmental and human health concern, as evident in the United States [5], and has proven deadly in Kodaikanal, India [6]. A recent conference organized by the Centre for Science and Environment (CSE) highlighted alarming levels of mercury contamination in India, primarily attributed to the discharge of mercury-bearing industrial effluents ranging from 290 nM to 1.36 uM, significantly exceeding prescribed Indian and WHO standards of 5 nM for drinking water and 50 nM for industrial effluents [7]. These cases collectively underscore the ongoing relevance of Hg2+ sensing for environmental protection.

Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and cold-vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy (CV-AAS) are extensively employed for the detection of Hg2+ ions, achieving high levels of sensitivity and precision [8]. Nonetheless, these techniques demand advanced analytical equipment, skilled operators, and complex sample preparation procedures, which constrain their practical application. Other methods have been explored, such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering, resonance Rayleigh scattering, colorimetry, and electrochemical sensing [9]. Among these, fluorescence assays exhibit significant advantages due to their high sensitivity, simplicity, rapid response, easy visualization, and real-time analysis capabilities [10]. To achieve optimal sensitivity and selectivity, fluorescence probe materials prepared through relatively simple methods are water-soluble, stable in a wide pH range, respond rapidly, and be applicable on-site. However, many reported fluorescent probes need these characteristics, limiting their applications. Tackling these issues is essential for creating a simple, rapid, and affordable analytical method that offers high sensitivity and selectivity. This highlights the importance of developing a fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection that is suitable for on-site use in complex matrices.CQDs have emerged as versatile fluorescent probes due to their tunable optical properties and high sensitivity in detecting various analytes [11]. Recent studies have demonstrated the successful synthesis of CQDs using microwave-assisted methods and their application in sensing metal ions, surfactants, and biomolecules with excellent selectivity and low detection limits [12,13].

In contrast, while significant progress has been made in improving specific aspects of CQDs-based Hg2+ detection [14,15], such as quantum yield enhancement [16,17], selectivity, and limit of reduction (LOD) reduction [18], there remains a dearth of research focusing on understanding the underlying interaction mechanisms between CQDs and Hg2+. While some studies have explored quenching mechanisms using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy techniques [19], there is a noticeable scarcity of novel approaches to comprehensively elucidate these interactions. Moreover, the practical application of CQDs in paper-based detection methods, which offer convenience and accessibility, has received limited attention [20]. Existing studies in this area often involve complex paper preparation techniques and meticulous analysis methodologies [21,22], suggesting a gap in developing streamlined and reliable paper-based detection methods utilizing CQDs. Thus, this research seeks to bridge these gaps by investigating novel strategies to understand CQDs-Hg2+ interaction mechanisms and enhancing the practical utility of CQDs-based probes, particularly in paper-based detection applications.

In this study, we synthesized CQDs using a one-pot microwave synthesis method, employing citric acid and urea as precursors. Optimization of synthesis parameters, including the amounts of citric acid and urea, as well as reaction time, was conducted to achieve high fluorescence intensity. The morphology and chemical structure of the synthesized CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–Vis spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were also performed to analyze the CQDs further. Upon excitation at 400 nm, the CQDs exhibited cyan emission at 520 nm, which was found to be rapidly quenched in the presence of Hg2+ ions. Notably, this quenching mechanism was thoroughly investigated using various techniques, including UV–Vis, FTIR, TEM, and agarose gel electrophoresis-based novel approach. Based on these findings, we propose a new fluorescence sensor utilizing CQDs for highly selective analysis of Hg2+. The developed method demonstrates excellent analytical performance with rapid response and high selectivity for detecting Hg2+ in various environmental water samples across different pH levels. Moreover, the visual observation can detect the significant fluorescence quenching effect through a simple procedure to develop a CQDs-based paper sensor, offering a practical and simple alternative to existing methods, as evidenced by the more visually impactful images depicting fluorescence quenching in the paper-based sensor.

Calcium (II) Chloride (CaCl2, ≥ 98.0 % ), Ferric (III) Nitrate nanohydrate (
, ≥ 98.0 % ), Lead (II) acetate (
, ≥ 99.5 % ), Manganese (II) acetate hexahydrate (
,
≥ 98 . 0 %
), Mercury (II) Chloride (HgCl2, ≥ 99.0 % ), Nickel (II) Chloride hexahydrate (
, ≥ 98.0 % ), and Zinc (II) Chloride (ZnCl2, ≥ 95.0 % ) were purchased from Merck Life Science Pvt. Ltd., India. Aluminum (III) nitrate nanohydrate (
, ≥ 98 % ) and Citric Acid (C6H8O7, ≥ 99.5 % ) were obtained from SRL, India. Sodium (I) Chloride (NaCl, ≥ 99.0 % ), Cadmium (II) chloride (CdCl2, ≥ 95.0 % ), Cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (
,
≥ 97 . 0 %
), Copper (II) Chloride dihydrate (
, ≥ 98.0 % ), Ferrous (II) sulfate heptahydrate (
, ≥ 99.0 % ), Magnesium (II) acetate tetrahydrate (
, ≥ 98.0 % ), and Urea (
, ≥ 99.5 % ) were obtained from Fisher Scientific, India. Fluorescence grade rhodamine B (
) was obtained from Merck as a reference dye. All the purchased chemicals and reagents were used without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized using one-step simple microprocessor-assisted synthesis [23]. We have taken 200 mg Citric acid and 400 mg Urea and denoted it as (2–4). In the synthesis process, the different combinations of weight ratios of citric acid and urea are taken as CQDs (2–4), CQDs (3–4), CQDs (4–4), and CQDs (5–4), and dissolved in double distilled water for optimal synthesis ratio. The mixture solutions were heated in a simple microwave oven for 240 s, brown color solution was obtained, and water was evaporated. The obtained CQDs precipitate was washed with distilled water thrice at 10000 rpm for 8 min at room temperature. To find out CQDs’ optimal synthesis time, fluorescence properties of CQDs (3–4) were also studied at the microwave reaction time of 120, 240, and 360 s, respectively.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was carried out on a Perkin Elmer Cary 5000 spectrometer with quartz cuvettes. Fluorescence measurements were performed using a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer, also with quartz cuvettes. The crystal structure of CQD samples was examined using a Rigaku Mini Flex-II XRD spectrometer, calibrated with Cu K α radiation (
λ = 0 . 154
nm
), operating at 15 mA current and 30 kV voltage. XRD measurements were conducted in the 5-80
∘
2 θ
range, employing a continuous scan with a step size of 0.02 ° and a scanning rate of 4 ° per minute. For XRD analysis, colloidal CQDs were drop-cast onto glass slides. XPS characterization was performed using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III instrument, and data analysis was done with CasaXPS software. Chemical functional group analysis was conducted with sample pellets prepared with KBr using a Perkin-Elmer Spectra II FTIR spectrometer. The morphology and size distribution of CQDs were analyzed using a JEOL JEM-3000F TEM instrument. Sample images and photographs were taken with a Canon PowerShot S95 digital camera. Zeta potential measurements of CQDs were made using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 instrument. Electrophoresis was conducted using a BIO-RAD electrophoresis power supply.

CQDs (3–4) were diluted in 5 ml DI water to
200
μ l
synthesized CQDs (3–4) and were utilized for fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ ions selectively at normal room temperature and pressure. The specific ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3++, Fe3++, and Na+) were prepared at a concentration of 100 μ M. A 1 ml aliquot of each ion solution was combined with 1 ml of diluted CQDs (3–4) and the fluorescence was recorded over a period of 7 min.

Fluorescence sensing experiments were performed to assess how different metal ions influence the interaction between Hg2+ ions and CQDs (3–4). The study involved mixing Hg2+ ions with metal ions including Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3++, Fe3++, and Na+, each at a concentration of 100 μ M. The alterations in fluorescence spectra of the CQDs (3–4) solution upon addition of Hg2+ ions were compared both in the presence of other metal ions and with Hg2+ ions alone.

The pH titration was carried out to examine the fluorescence spectra stability of CQDs (3–4) across different pH levels. The aim was to determine the best pH range for the detection of Hg2+ ions at a concentration of 100 μ M. NaOH and HCl were used to vary the pH range from acidic 1 pH to a basic medium of 13 pH.

The fluorescence sensing of CQDs (3–4) was studied in the 0.1–14 μ M
ions concentration range using fluorescence spectroscopy, with readings taken 7 min post-mixing. A linear calibration curve was estimated by observing the change in fluorescence intensity
( 1 − I /
I
0
)
against Hg2+ ions concentrations. The limit of quantification (LOQ) and LOD were determined by the equation
LOQ =
10 σ
m
and
LOD =
3 σ
m
[24], respectively, where σ denotes the standard deviation calculated from three blank samples and m represents the slope obtained from the linear plot of fluorescence intensity against
ion concentration. Reproducibility and repeatability were assessed through three repetitions to evaluate the fluorescence sensing of
ions standard solution with CQDs (3–4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Agarose gel electrophoresis of negatively charged CQDs (3–4)
ce:section.ce:para:
This experiment used FTIR, DLS, and TEM to explain the sensing mechanism. In the agarose gel electrophoresis experiment, a 2% agarose gel was prepared by dissolving agarose powder in Tris Acetate EDTA (TAE) buffer and cooling the solution to approximately 50 °C. This experiment utilized undiluted synthesized CQDs (3–4) to get high fluorescent contrast. The gel was cast in a tray with four wells designated A, B, C, and D. The electrophoresis was conducted by submerging the gel in an electrophoresis chamber filled with TAE buffer and running it at a constant voltage of 120 V for 20 min. Post-electrophoresis, the gel was placed on a UV transilluminator to visualize the fluorescence pattern of the negatively charged CQDs (3–4) within the agarose gel. Images were captured for documentation, allowing for the analysis of the migration pattern and distribution of the CQDs (3–4) in each well. The distinct loading of different concentrations of
ions in wells B, C, and D aimed to observe any variations in the migration pattern of CQDs (3–4) under the influence of varying
concentrations. This focused experimental design provides insights into the interaction between CQDs (3–4) and
ions.

The paper-based sensor was fabricated utilizing a solution of diluted CQDs (3–4). The coating solution was prepared by adding 200 μ L of the CQDs (3–4) solution to 4800 μ L of deionized water. Whatman Grade 1 cellulose filter paper served as the substrate. A large petri dish (200 mm diameter and 20 mm height) contained 50 mL of the diluted CQDs solution, into which the Whatman paper was immersed for 5 min. Subsequently, the coated paper sensor was carefully dried at 60 °C for 2 h in a hot air oven. 50 μ L each of the (
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
,
, and
) solution was added to the CQDs (3–4)-embedded paper sensor to assess its selectivity and sensing performance at room temperature. The fabricated paper sensor was positioned 30 cm from a 15-watt fluorescence UV-lamp emitting light at 385 nm. Any fluorescence variations in the paper sensor were systematically monitored using a digital camera. These procedures were repeated three times to ensure the reliability and repeatability of the experimental results.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs (2–4), CQDs (3–4), CQDs (4–4), and CQDs (5–4) are depicted in Fig. S1 (a). Notably, all major peak emission wavelengths for CQDs occur around 520 nm under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The fluorescence intensity is most pronounced for CQDs (3–4). This enhancement is attributed to the CO-NH
2
functional group, forming a cyan fluorescent center within the CQDs. The varying ratio of citric acid to urea in the synthesis process dictates the prevalence of specific functional groups, with higher citric acid favoring COOH and dominant urea favoring NH
2
groups on the CQDs surface, thereby influencing fluorescence intensity and peak emission wavelengths. Optimization of reaction time is crucial, as insufficient time results in incomplete reactions and low fluorescence. In contrast, excessive time leads to aggregation and precipitation of CQDs, also causing reduced fluorescence intensity. Fig. S1 (b) highlights that the optimal weight ratio for the highest fluorescence intensity is observed in CQDs (3–4), with a reaction time of 240 s. The synthesized CQDs (3–4) showed high stability for up to 3 months, quantum dots remained water dispersible, and stable fluorescence was observed (Fig. S4).

In the UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis of our synthesized CQDs (3–4), three distinct absorption peaks were identified (Fig. 1 (a)). The peak observed at 278 nm corresponds to
π →
π
∗
transitions of carbon–carbon bonds in aromatic structures, indicating the presence of aromatic domains within the CQDs [25]. The second peak, found at 338 nm, represents the
n →
π
∗
transition, typically associated with carbonyl and amine functional groups on the CQD’s surface, implying potential reactive sites [26]. The final and major peak of CQDs (3–4), detected at 407 nm, may be attributed to the interactions between the surface states and the molecular center of the CQDs [27].

The fluorescence spectrum analysis (Fig. 1 (b)) revealed broad peaks, suggesting an overlap of emission wavelengths at 520 nm and 450 nm in CQDs (3–4). This suggests the presence of diverse energy levels or a range in particle sizes within the CQDs. Notably, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at 520 nm, corresponding to an excitation wavelength of 400 nm for CQDs (3–4) under standard room temperature conditions. This observation is attributed to the influence of surface functional groups or defects in the CQDs [28]. Furthermore, a discernible shoulder hump at 450 nm in the spectrum indicates an excitation wavelength-dependent emission feature, likely arising from
π
∗
→ n
electronic transitions of surface-bound C=C and C-NH2 bonds, indicative of specific molecular configurations or interactions [29]. The fluorescence intensity exhibited an initial increase with excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 400 nm, followed by a decrease, suggesting a reduction in the emission efficiency of the CQDs. Notably, the fluorescence peak of CQDs (3–4) exhibited a shift from 510 to 530 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 380 to 460 nm. A similar phenomenon is often observed in the other CQDs, indicating that the excitation-dependent emissions are a unique feature of CQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs (3–4) is also calculated to be 45.03% using the relative method with rhodamine B as the reference fluorescence dye (Fig. S5) [30].

The XPS analysis was employed to explore the surface chemical compositions of CQDs (3–4). The XPS analysis of the prepared CQDs (3–4) illustrated in Fig. 1 (c) yielded insights into the material’s elemental composition and bonding characteristics. The survey spectrum in Fig. 1 (c) revealed a noticeable signal for C 1s and O 1s, while no signal was detected for N 1s, suggesting that nitrogen is present in minute quantities or absent from the surface of CQDs (3–4).

The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, shown in Fig. 1 (d), provided a detailed analysis of the carbon bonding environment. The well-fitted spectra displayed several subpeaks, highlighting the presence of various carbon–carbon and carbon–oxygen bonds. Specifically, the presence of peaks at 284.6 eV was linked to the presence of carbon–carbon (C-C) and carbon–carbon double (C=C) bonds characteristic of graphitic sp
2
carbon structures, while the peak at 286.3 eV suggested the presence of carbon–oxygen (C-O) bonds in hydroxylated carbon compounds. Moreover, the peak observed at 288.8 eV was associated with the oxygen–carbon–oxygen (O-C=O) bonds typical of carboxylated carbon species [31].

In Fig. 1 (e), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s provided additional details on the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CQDs (3–4). The three peaks at 531.9, 532.8, and 533.5 eV were identified as C=O, O-C=O, and C-O, respectively [32]. The XPS results illustrated that CQDs (3–4) were predominantly composed of carbon and oxygen elements. The presence of abundant functional groups, notably
− COOH
and
− OH
, was evident on the surface, indicating a complex and diverse molecular composition suitable for cation Hg
2 +
interaction.

The FTIR spectroscopy analysis offered insights into the nature of functional groups present on the surfaces of the synthesized CQDs (3–4) (Fig. 1 (f)). A broad peak was identified at 3435 cm−1, associated with the stretching of O-H and N-H bonds, indicative of hydroxyl and amine groups, respectively [33]. Further analysis revealed a peak at 2922 cm−1, associated with the C-H bond stretching, suggesting the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbon groups. Peaks within the 1670–1620 cm−1 region correspond to C=C and CO stretching vibrations, providing evidence for the existence of unsaturated carbon–carbon bonds and carbonyl groups, respectively [34]. The peak at 1403 cm−1, attributed to the stretching of C-N [35], indicates the presence of amine groups. Furthermore, a small peak around 1100 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O, with additional bond stretching of the C-N skeleton observed within the absorption region of 1100–1003 cm−1 [36]. The absorption region between 812–665 cm−1 is derived from the out-of-plane deformation vibration of C-H in cyclic alkenes with internal double bonds [37]. These FTIR results demonstrate that the surfaces of the CQDs (3–4) are coated with hydrophilic groups, specifically hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, which account for the observed excellent aqueous solubility. This suggests that our synthesis process effectively functionalized the CQDs (3–4), making them ideal for potential applications in aqueous environments.

The XRD analysis of the synthesized CQDs (3–4) revealed crystal phase, structural, and compositional details (Fig. S2 (a)). A notable broad peak at around 23° in the
2 θ
range indicates the presence of graphitic-like carbon material, as it aligns with the (002) plane corresponding to the JCPDS card number 65-6212 [38]. The broadness of the peak implies the existence of a small crystalline core structure within the CQDs (3–4), likely due to the small size of graphitic domains [39]. Moreover, the XRD pattern suggests the presence of an amorphous functional group surrounding this carbon core, contributing to a more disordered structure. An intriguing aspect of this amorphous region is the potential inclusion of various functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups [28]. These groups enhance the water-solubility of the CQDs (3–4), broadening their application scope. The XRD analysis suggests that the CQDs (3–4) have a core–shell structure, with a graphitic-like core enveloped by an amorphous shell abundant in various functional groups, establishing a foundation for their extensive potential applications.

The morphology and particle size distribution of the synthesized CQDs (3–4) were directly visualized through TEM. As seen in Fig. S2 (b), these CQDs (3–4) are predominantly spherical and exhibit a high degree of uniformity in their structure, with good dispersion in the solution and an absence of noticeable aggregation. This observation suggests a notable stability of the CQDs (3–4), a key factor for their practical usage in diverse applications. The size analysis showed an average size of
2 . 85 ± 0 . 55
nm
. As presented in the inset of Fig. S2 (b), the particle size distribution histogram illustrates a narrow size distribution, ranging between 2–4 nm. This consistent size distribution further reinforces the uniformity of the synthesis process, potentially contributing to the observed dispersion properties.

A selectivity assay was conducted to assess the selectivity of CQDs (3–4) in metal ion detection. Various metal ions, including Hg
2 +
, Mg
2 +
, Ca2+, Mn
2 +
, Fe2+, Co
2 +
, Ni
2 +
, Cu
2 +
, Zn
2 +
, Cd
2 +
, Pb
2 +
, Al3+, Fe3+, and Na
+
were introduced to the fluorescent CQDs (3–4) as shown in Fig. 2 (a-b). Among these ions, only the CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
solution exhibited decreased fluorescence intensity within 7 min of observation, indicating the specific detection capability of CQDs (3–4) towards Hg
2 +
ions. Within 7 min, mixing Hg
2 +
ions led to the quenching of CQDs (3–4) without inducing aggregation or precipitation, highlighting the selective and rapid response of CQDs (3–4). As depicted in the bar graph of selectivity (Fig. 2 (c)), the response of CQDs (3–4) towards Hg
2 +
ions stands out distinctly compared to other metal ions. Specifically, only Hg
2 +
ions demonstrated reduced fluorescence intensity (I/I
0
), while the values for other metal ions remained close to those of CQDs (3–4). The fluorescence signal of the solution containing the CQDs (3–4) and Hg
2 +
ions mixture was continuously monitored at regular intervals with fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3 (b)).

In Fig. 3 (a), it is observed that the fluorescence intensity (I/I
0
) of the synthesized CQDs (3–4) increases below a pH of 6. This increase is attributed to the interaction of CQDs (3–4) facilitated by hydrogen bonding due to the process of converting carboxylate anions into their protonated forms [40]. The proportion remains consistent across the pH range of 6–9, suggesting the sustained stability of CQDs (3–4). Upon addition of Hg
2 +
ions, a notable decline in fluorescence intensity is noted within the pH range of 6–9, indicating the quenching of CQDs (3–4) by Hg
2 +
ions. When the pH exceeds 9, a decline occurs, potentially attributed to the buildup of colloidal Hg(OH)
2
[41]. These findings suggest that the pH range of 6–9 is suitable for monitoring Hg
2 +
ions with CQDs (3–4).

Fluorescence sensing was performed to determine the selectivity of CQDs (3–4) for Hg
2 +
ions amidst other metal ions in the assay. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs (3–4) and Hg
2 +
ions reaction product was studied in the presence of different metal ions at a concentration of 100 μ M. The inclusion of other metal ions in the assay did not elicit any notable alteration in the fluorescence intensity of the resulting solution (Fig. 2 (c)). This finding suggests that different metal ions do not affect fluorescence-based detection of Hg
2 +
ions using CQDs (3–4). This result underscores the high selectivity of CQDs (3–4) as fluorescence sensors for detecting Hg
2 +
ions.

During the linear range examination, a gradual reduction in the fluorescence peak was observed at approximately 520 nm as the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions increased. This observation suggests a direct correlation between the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions and the fluorescence quenching effect on CQDs (3–4) (Fig. 3 (c)). The linear relationship observed between the fluorescence intensity (1-I/I
0
) and Hg
2 +
ions concentration, as shown in Fig. 3 (d), further corroborated the dependence of CQDs (3–4) fluorescence quenching on the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions. A linear regression model was applied to determine the association between fluorescence and the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions across the linear span of 2 to 9 μ M. The obtained regression coefficient of 0.99861 indicates a relationship between fluorescence and Hg
2 +
ion concentration within this defined range. The calculated LOD for CQDs (3–4) was 1.8 nM, signifying the minimum concentration of Hg
2 +
ions reliably detectable. Additionally, the LOQ was determined to be 6 nM. To contextualize our findings, a comparison was conducted between the outcomes of this study and prior investigation regarding the utilization of fluorescence CQDs as a mercury sensor, as outlined in Table 1.

The synthesized CQDs (3–4) were examined for their sensing mechanism toward Hg
2 +
ions. Thus far, FTIR, UV–Vis, TEM, and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy have been utilized to study the sensing mechanism. The electrophoresis technique is used for the first time to further support the sensing mechanism in separate sub-sections. FTIR spectroscopy established the presence of functional groups, specifically carboxylic groups, on the CQDs (3–4) surface, which are known for their ability to attach to metal ions, demonstrating their capability for Hg
2 +
ion sensing [42]. Additionally, a zeta potential study demonstrated the negative charge on CQDs (3–4) with a negative potential of −12.6 mV, indicative of an affinity for positively charged Hg
2 +
ions (Fig. S3).

The FTIR spectra provided evidence to these observations, showing that the N-H/O-H stretching mode in CQDs (3–4) shifted from 3440 to 3540 cm−1 in the presence of Hg
2 +
ions, indicating an interaction (Fig. 1 (f)). Furthermore, the C-H (2922 cm−1) and C-O/C-N (1020–1080 cm−1) peaks disappeared entirely. The C=C/C=O (1620 cm−1) peak related to the carboxylic group thinned out with the introduction of Hg
2 +
ions. This suggests that the carboxylic group (-COOH) in CQDs (3–4), with its propensity to lose an electron and become ionized (-COO
−
), engages strongly with Hg
2 +
ions, leading to an acceleration in the electron transfer process. This process likely results in the formation of an unstable carbon–mercury compound that further decomposes into Hg-O [36]. The CQDs (3–4) + Hg
2 +
FTIR spectra exhibited the Hg-O bond (450 cm−1) [43], corroborating the complex formation and offering an explanation for the observed fluorescence quenching behavior.

In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (3–4), a characteristic absorption peak at 338 nm was observed (Fig. 4 (c)), which is associated with the
n →
π
∗
electronic transition. The introduction of Hg
2 +
ions led to a decline in the intensity of this peak, which suggests the formation of a CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
complex. Moreover, the main absorbance peak at 407 nm shifted to 412 nm, a phenomenon reminiscent of nanoparticle aggregation [44], indicating the formation of the CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
complex and resulting in fluorescence quenching.

TEM analysis further supported these findings by revealing that in the presence of Hg
2 +
ions, CQDs (3–4) tend to aggregate (Fig. 4 (a-b)). This aggregation leads to the formation of a CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
complex that alters the electronic structure of CQDs (3–4), influencing exciton distribution and accelerating non-radiative electron/hole recombination through an effective transfer process, which in turn results in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [39]. Based on the above studies conducted to determine the mechanism, it is evident that a ground state complex forms through specific interactions, confirming the presence of a static quenching mechanism in the system [45]. Concurrently, we observed a constant peak position of the fluorescence curve throughout our sensing experiments.

The agarose gel electrophoresis conducted in this study was strategically devised to unravel the intricate interaction between negatively charged CQDs and cationic Hg
2 +
. The migration dynamics of fluorescent CQDs (3–4) were tracked across varying concentrations of Hg
2 +
, revealing a pattern that sheds light on the complex formation between CQDs and Hg
2 +
.

As elucidated in Fig. 4 (d), a noteworthy trend emerged, depicting a gradual increase in the migration of CQDs (3–4) as the concentration of Hg
2 +
decreased. The observation in well B was of particular significance, where the Hg
2 +
concentration peaked at 10 mM, resulting in minimal migration of the CQDs (3–4) and a more concentrated band of CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
complex. The complex can be observed to be stuck in the well. This observation suggests the formation of sizeable complexes between CQDs and Hg
2 +
, effectively impeding their mobility through the gel. In contrast, in well A (absence of Hg
2 +
), the majority of free CQDs (3–4) traversed the gel, creating a diffused band of CQDs (3–4).

A comparative analysis of migration patterns in wells C and D relative to well B offers insights into the complex formation between CQDs (3–4) and Hg
2 +
. Well C, featuring a lower Hg
2 +
concentration of 1 mM, exhibited increased migration compared to well B, indicating that a less complex formation led to relatively higher mobility. Similarly, well D, with a concentration of 10 uM, displayed increased mobility compared to both well B and well C. The migration pattern supports the hypothesis that the formation of CQDs (3–4)- Hg
2 +
complexes is influenced by the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions.

The results derived from agarose gel electrophoresis furnish compelling evidence for forming CQDs (3–4)-Hg
2 +
complexes. Notably, this study, to the best of our knowledge, marks the first-time use of gel electrophoresis to gain valuable insights into the interaction dynamics between CQDs and Hg
2 +
, opening avenues for its application in fluorescent nanoparticle sensing mechanisms and contributing to the broader field of nanotechnology.

The paper sensor was developed through a simple technique outlined in the method section, offering an advantageous alternative to the more intricate printing processes. This simple method ensures an even distribution of CQDs (3–4) on the Whatman paper surface. Testing immediately after the drying process exhibited a swift response to the introduction of target ions, particularly evident in the pronounced darkening effect on the sensor’s surface in the presence of Hg
2 +
ions (Fig. 5 (a-b)). Notably, this darkening effect became more pronounced after 5 min as the surface dried, emphasizing the dynamic nature of the sensor’s response. This darkening persisted consistently after 24 h and even after seven days, underscoring the robust stability of the paper sensor over an extended period.

The selectivity of the paper sensor was further examined by introducing various ions at a concentration of 100 μ M. Among them, only Hg
2 +
demonstrated a quenching effect, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a-b), confirming the high selectivity of the paper sensor for toxic mercury ions. The visible range of the paper sensor was assessed by varying the concentration of Hg
2 +
ions under a 385 nm UV lamp. Notably, concentrations of 1 mM, 0.5 mM, and 100 μ M Hg
2 +
ions exhibited decreasing levels of darkening. In contrast, even lower concentrations (1 μ M, 5 μ M, and 10 μ M) resulted in minimal darkening of the paper surface (Fig. 5 (c-d)). This concentration-dependent response highlights the sensitivity of the paper sensor. The results demonstrate that a 100 μ M concentration can be effectively measured using this paper sensor. Its simplicity of fabrication, stability, and high selectivity make this paper sensor a promising candidate for environmental monitoring and mercury contamination detection applications.

This study demonstrates the strong potential of CQDs for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions, providing a rapid and efficient solution for mercury sensing. The one-step microwave synthesis using urea and citric acid as precursors produced well-dispersed CQDs, which exhibited remarkable stability for three months and maintained excellent performance without requiring complex procedures. These CQDs showed high selectivity and allowed for interference-free, rapid detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of 1.8 nM. The development of a very simple, paper-based sensor for visual mercury ion detection also offers a practical solution for resource-limited settings. A detailed mechanistic study, utilizing UV–Vis, FTIR, TEM, and for the first time, agarose gel electrophoresis, confirmed the static quenching mechanism through the formation of the CQDs-Hg2+ complex and its migration behavior. This highlights the usefulness of agarose gel electrophoresis for mechanism studies involving complex formation, especially in fluorescence probes. Despite the small sample size and limited real-world testing, this study provides a solid foundation for developing cost-effective and portable mercury detection tools. Future research should focus on exploring the performance of CQDs in more complex matrices and real-world environments. The detailed static mechanism uncovered in this study can serve as a groundwork for further mechanistic investigations. These insights have the potential to significantly advance the detection and remediation of heavy metal contamination in water sources.
